planting.note: Sow seed in early fall when soil temperatures are 50–65°F, or in early spring in moist, lean to moderately fertile, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; creeping red fescue spreads by rhizomes to fill in bare spots, unlike sheep's fescue. It establishes readily and requires minimal fertilization — over-fertilizing produces lush, disease-prone growth.

directions: Sow in lean to moderately fertile, well-drained to moist soil in full sun to part shade; creeping red fescue is the most versatile and widely used fine fescue in lawn and groundcover applications — a cool-season grass of particular value in three difficult situations: shaded lawns under trees (where Kentucky bluegrass and ryegrass fail), dry slopes and banks with minimal irrigation, and low-maintenance meadow and naturalizing applications. Unlike sheep's fescue (F. ovina), which forms non-spreading bunches, creeping red fescue spreads by rhizomes to fill in bare spots and form a continuous cover over time. Its fine, hair-like, dark-green leaves create a soft, elegant texture and remain green in cool weather. It tolerates more shade than any other common cool-season lawn grass, making it indispensable in lawn blends for partially shaded sites. It is the dominant grass in many premium fine fescue lawn blends, often combined with hard fescue (F. brevipila) and chewings fescue (F. rubra subsp. commutata) for all-fescue, no-mow or low-mow lawn applications that require only 1–2 mowings per season.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in very well-drained, lean, low-fertility, alkaline to neutral desert soil in full sun; Baja fairy duster is a desert plant that requires excellent drainage and is damaged at temperatures below 28°F. In zone 9 it dies back in hard winters but resprouts vigorously from established roots. Supplemental watering every 2–3 weeks in summer promotes repeat blooming.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Baja fairy duster is a desert shrub native to the Baja California peninsula and Sonoran Desert that produces clouds of brilliant, brushy, red powder-puff flowers consisting almost entirely of long, bright-red stamens with no conspicuous petals — a classic desert flower form that has evolved specifically for hummingbird pollination. It blooms heavily in spring and fall with sporadic flowers between, providing critical nectar for migrating and resident hummingbirds during the desert seasons when few other plants are in bloom. The fine, bipinnate, semi-evergreen foliage has a soft, feathery texture. More cold-sensitive than the pink fairy duster (Calliandra eriophylla, which is already well-known in Arizona desert gardens), it is best suited to the warmer deserts and coastal warm-winter climates of zones 9b–11. Outstanding combined with desert willow (Chilopsis linearis), autumn sage (Salvia greggii), and other hummingbird-attracting desert plants.

common_name: Yellow Bird of Paradise (Desert Bird of Paradise)

planting.note: Scarify seeds or soak overnight before sowing; plant container-grown shrubs in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun. Yellow bird of paradise reseeds prolifically and can naturalize aggressively in desert climates — verify local invasive status before planting in the Southwest. It dies back in hard freezes but resprouts from established roots in zone 8.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; yellow bird of paradise is a native of Argentina that has naturalized extensively in the American Southwest and Mexico, prized for its brilliant, eye-catching flowers — large, canary-yellow petals surrounding long, dramatic, brilliant-red stamens that extend 2–3 inches beyond the petals in striking contrast. The flowers appear in loose, upright clusters at the branch tips from summer through fall — a spectacularly colorful display during the dry season when many desert plants are dormant. The compound, ferny, blue-green foliage is attractive on its own. It is among the most drought-tolerant ornamental shrubs available, requiring virtually no water once established in desert climates, but can be grown throughout zones 8–11 with well-drained soil. Caution: it reseeds prolifically and has naturalized invasively in parts of the Sonoran Desert and adjacent areas; check local invasive status before planting in Arizona and southern New Mexico.

planting.note: Sow seeds or plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, sandy or gravelly, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; perennial sea lavender develops a deep taproot and resents transplanting once established — site carefully. It tolerates salt spray, coastal conditions, and alkaline soils better than most perennials. Do not over-water; wet winter conditions cause root rot.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, sandy or gravelly, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; perennial sea lavender is the long-lived perennial statice — a superior plant to the familiar annual Limonium sinuatum of florist bouquets, producing massive, airy clouds of tiny, papery, lavender-blue to violet flowers in billowing sprays from July through September. Each individual flower consists of two parts: an outer papery calyx in a rich purple and an inner true flower in cream, both of which retain their color when dried — making the plant outstanding for everlasting dried arrangements. The flower sprays rise 2–3 feet above a rosette of large, leathery, basal leaves, the whole creating an airy, haze-like display in the late-summer border. It is exceptionally tolerant of coastal salt spray, dry conditions, and alkaline soils — thriving in the coastal gardens of the Northeast and mid-Atlantic where it can self-sow into rock crevices and wall tops. Excellent as a long-lasting cut flower (fresh or dried) and as a companion plant for late-season color with ornamental grasses and salvias.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Australian rosemary is very drought-tolerant once established and sensitive to waterlogged soils. It tolerates coastal salt spray and alkaline conditions. Lightly shear after each flowering flush to keep plants compact and bushy.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Australian rosemary is a coastal Australian native shrub from the mint family that closely resembles culinary rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) in appearance — with narrow, whorled, needle-like leaves, a similar upright to spreading habit, and small tubular flowers — but is not used as a culinary herb and is generally more drought-tolerant, salt-tolerant, and adaptable than true rosemary. The flowers are white to pale lavender, produced nearly continuously through the year in mild coastal climates, providing a steady nectar source. It is one of the most reliable evergreen flowering shrubs for hot, dry, coastal zones 8–11, tolerating conditions of salt spray, poor soil, and extended drought that defeat most ornamentals. Widely planted in California coastal landscaping as a rosemary substitute where salt exposure makes true rosemary struggle. The cultivar 'Wynyabbie Gem' has more consistent lavender-mauve flowers; 'Morning Light' has cream-variegated foliage.
